text: Hmm, engineers understand the technology the product is built with, designers understand the purpose of the product. The trick is to keep both communicating whilst in their respective swim lanes.

author: @thedevilinthecircuit
text: The book *The Design of Everyday Things* by Don Norman addresses all this stuff; Norman asserts that if something fails, it's not the user's fault--it's the design.  The design of something should leave no questions about how it is to be used.  It should be intuitive by design.

author: @dancolquhoun5593

author: @MacsMachines
text:
Great video Rob, very interesting take on it (as ever). 

I have a number of 32 pounder Sussex iron cannons…thanks for raising awareness of some of their contributions to engineering (tread mills also were used to run furnace bellows in the great drought of 1743 when their rivers dried up for powering water wheels!)... I did a video on the subject and it only got 25 views 😂 (I can get many thousands for a motorbike going up a road)

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Regarding engineers use physics... Ever heard of the continuum assumption? It  gets utilised a lot in the study/understanding of how fluid behave.

 Put simply it utterly ignores the idea of atoms, and assumes fluids can be divided into infinitely small volumes.

It's done to make the maths easier, and usually results a model that is fairly accurate to reality.. 

physicists will often quake at how engineers are sometimes prepaired to willfully  ignore knowledge

author: @custos3249
text: I've often wondered if we were to ever generate new titles for various fields to both make more sense on face value (clearer what the person does) and potentially cooler sounding, what would titles change to. For engineering, best I've come up with is "materials strategist." And for chemistry, probably a bit more off the mark than I'm aware, but "molecular electrician" made me laugh.

author: @rayg436

author: @deltonlomatai2309
text: He built the castle in a year?  That is amazing.  Engineers for massive projects have to over come the logistic and organize constraints that affect time to completion.  They did it in a year.  Hats off to Henry's engineers.  Here in America before a project of large scale gets started it takes 2 to 3 years to get passed the environmental impact which is part of the social-political constraints.  Maybe Elon Musk learned a lot from the old engineers.  The rockets he builds are made of steel and not aluminum which is easier and cheaper to work.  NASA project have a lot of pork.  For government projects to funding money has to get kick back to the states.  Something that Elon does not have to do.  Thank you for you video and insights.

author: @coachgeo
text: My grandfather was retired engineer (built TVA dams) working at a Univ. grounds, buildings etc.  Token-ish job I suspect.   They put in new buildings and asked him to design/draw up where to put sidewalks.   And asked..... and asked.... after a few years... tired of asking they asked again....   he marched them up the top most view looking out the building.....  pointed down to the grass below that had finally grown full.... it clearly showed where everyone walked....It was  like cow paths in the grass down bellow.......... "you put them THERE " as he pointed at the paths.    (least that is how I heard the story lol.  Sadly never met him)  Anyway.... thought you might like the story too for it fits you point...... Engineering is designs for the people.

author: @livingladolcevita7318
text: You make a good point about ceiling height,, my rooms are about 8 ft high, so I have put false ceilings in with extra insulation and I still have 7 ft.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: High ceilings help for smoke from fires . The smoke can form a layer above the heads like a cloud layer and vent out through holes high up . Better designed fireplaces make the requirement redundant.

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text: It's good to get more people looking to past mechanisms. Technology tends to push us away from it for mass produced over-complicated and depdendant devices instead of more reliable mechanisms which just had a bit more thought about simplification with clever tricks. There was a bit of it in old toys, e.g. 1 motor driving 4 walking legs on a toy robot and mechanically storing the energy from winding it up lol, that could surely be taken so much further, just for random devices individually like a toaster if you want a workout lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i agree mate - there is so much we can do but somehow we seem to have been convinced we can't

replies.author: @ToninFightsEntropy
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering We seemed to atleast be going in the right direction with calculators having solar cells in them! I'm surprised that didn't pick up for more things.. or even a little hand-squeezey thing could be more than enough to power a digital camera...
Supercapacitors should be making this kind of thing possible for so many electric devices now, but I still don't understand why we moved away from atleast temporarily storing energy in springs/coils.

author: @peterkent2138
text: Engineers, in my opinion, are vastly underrated and under appreciated. All of the things that we take for granted have been designed and manufactured by engineers. Most of the medical advances are in fact engineering advanced bought about by scientists and engineers. We are the corner stone of civilisation!
